title: Carrageenan-bath acrylic marbling
subtitle: The European marbled-paper tradition using a thickened size and floating acrylic colours
excerpt: European paper marbling floats diluted acrylic paints on a thickened bath of carrageenan seaweed extract. The colours stay on the surface, can be manipulated with combs and styluses, and are lifted onto mordanted paper in a single pull. The technique produces the patterned endpapers associated with hand-bound books.
sourceNotes: C. W. Woolnough, The Art of Marbling (1853, 1881), is the primary English-language public-domain source for European marbling methods. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/artofmarbling00wool. The carrageenan sizing method is the modern-standard version of Woolnough's 'gum tragacanth size'; the technique is synthesised from multiple historical and craft references.

projectSchedule.title: Mix carrageenan size and mordant the paper
projectSchedule.body: Mix carrageenan size one day in advance so bubbles settle. Mordant all sheets with alum solution and allow to dry completely before marbling.

projectSchedule.title: Float colours, pattern, and lift prints
projectSchedule.body: Float diluted acrylic paints on the settled size, create patterns with a comb or stylus, and lift each print by lowering mordanted paper onto the surface.

glossaryTerms.definition: A thickened bath made from carrageenan, a seaweed-derived polysaccharide. The viscous surface holds acrylic paint droplets without them sinking or merging, allowing pattern-making before the print is lifted.
Bile salts extracted from cattle. A few drops added to acrylic marbling paint reduces its surface tension, allowing the paint to spread across the carrageenan size rather than beading up.
Paper treated with a dilute alum solution before marbling. The alum mordant causes the floating paint to bond to the sheet fibres when the sheet is lifted from the size.

content.text: Carrageenan-bath marbling is the technique behind the patterned endpapers of hand-bound books. Diluted acrylic paints treated with
and float without sinking. A comb or stylus draws the drops into patterns. A sheet of
is lowered onto the pattern and lifts it in a single pull. Each print is unrepeatable.

attrs.title: Acrylic paint disposal
attrs.body: Let spent acrylic paint water dry in a bucket before disposal rather than pouring down household drains; wear gloves when handling ox gall.

items.name: Marbling tray, at least A5, ideally A4
Paper mordanted with alum solution, fully dried
Potassium alum + water for mordanting
items.qty: 1 tbsp alum per 500 ml warm water

items.name: Newspaper strips for skimming between prints

content.text: Blend two teaspoons of carrageenan powder into one litre of cold water using a hand blender. The powder clumps if added to warm water directly. Blend for two minutes until fully dissolved. Let the size stand for at least four hours, ideally overnight, to allow air bubbles to settle. Bubbles on the size surface cause pinholes in the print. Before marbling, skim the surface once with a strip of newspaper to remove remaining bubbles.

Dissolve one tablespoon of potassium alum in 500 ml of warm water. Wearing gloves, sponge the solution over one face of each sheet. Allow to dry completely. The mordanted face is the face that must contact the size during printing.

Dilute each colour with water to approximately the consistency of skimmed milk. Add two to three drops of ox gall per tablespoon of paint and stir. Test each colour: hold a loaded eyedropper 50 mm above the size and release one drop. It should spread into a circle 30 to 50 mm wide. If it beads up, add more ox gall. If it spreads instantly to a thin film, dilute further with water. Each colour may need different proportions; test before the full session.

Pour the settled size into the tray to 30 to 40 mm depth. Drop colours from 20 mm above the surface in whatever arrangement you choose. Each new drop pushes previous drops outward. Once the surface is covered, draw a comb or skewer through the colours steadily and evenly. Slow strokes create smooth curves; fast strokes create tighter marks. Lift the print immediately after combing; the pattern drifts and softens after a minute or two.

Hold the mordanted sheet at the short edges. Lower it face-down onto the size, starting at one end and lowering progressively across the length to avoid trapping air. Let it rest for three to four seconds. Lift by one corner, peeling it off in one smooth motion. Rinse under a gentle cold-water tap and lay flat to dry. Skim the size with a newspaper strip before the next print.

items.symptom: The paint sinks rather than floating on the size
items.cause: Insufficient ox gall, or the paint is too heavy relative to the size.
items.fix: Add ox gall one drop at a time and retest after each addition. If the paint still sinks, dilute further with water. Some very heavily pigmented colours are inherently dense and cannot be corrected with ox gall alone; try a different brand or colour.

items.symptom: The print comes up pale or incomplete in patches
items.cause: The paper was not fully mordanted, or the mordanted face was not the face that touched the size.
items.fix: Re-mordant the paper and allow to dry completely. The mordanted face should feel very slightly stiffer than the reverse. Always lower mordanted-face-down.

items.symptom: The combed pattern smears during lifting
items.cause: The sheet was not lowered flat, trapping air bubbles that pushed colours aside.
items.fix: Lower the sheet from one end progressively rather than dropping it flat onto the surface.
